question_text: A diverging lens always produces images that are virtual, upright, and smaller than the object. Explain why a diverging lens cannot produce a real image.
correct_answer: A diverging lens spreads out light rays, making them appear to come from a point behind the lens. The light rays never actually meet (converge) at a real point, so they cannot form a real image that can be projected.
explanation: Diverging lenses push light rays apart, so the rays never actually come together at a point in front of the lens. Since real images need light rays to actually meet at a point, a diverging lens cannot make one—the rays only appear to meet when traced backwards.
